rows: Interface that all concrete products must implement
Base class; declares the factory method and contains business logic. Never directly creates concrete products.
Subclass that overrides the factory method to return a specific product

content.title: UML Structure
content.body:
<<interface>>
  Product
  + operation()
      ▲           ▲
      │           │
ConcreteProductA  ConcreteProductB

  Creator
  + factoryMethod(): Product   ← THE factory method
  + someOperation()            ← uses factoryMethod() internally
      ▲                ▲
      │                │
ConcreteCreatorA    ConcreteCreatorB
+ factoryMethod()   + factoryMethod()
  → new ProductA      → new ProductB

The factory method does **not** have to create a new instance every time. It can return an object from a **cache**, an **object pool**, or any other existing source — the client never knows the difference.

flashcards.q: What are the four participants in the Factory Method pattern?
flashcards.a: **Product** (the interface), **Concrete Product** (the implementation), **Creator** (declares factory method + business logic), **Concrete Creator** (overrides factory method to return a specific product).

flashcards.q: What is the only place in the Factory Method pattern where concrete class names appear?
flashcards.a: In the application's initialization/configuration code — the single location that reads config and instantiates the correct Concrete Creator. Everywhere else uses only abstract types.

flashcards.q: Can the factory method return an existing object instead of creating a new one?
flashcards.a: Yes. The factory method can return objects from a cache, an object pool, or any other source. The client doesn't know or care if it received a brand-new or recycled instance.
